title: Train for the Worst, Plan for the Best: Understanding Token Ordering in Masked Diffusions

reviews.summary: This paper studies a fundamental tradeoff in masked diffusion models (MDMs) for discrete data: harder training subproblems versus flexible, order-agnostic inference. The authors provide average-case hardness results showing that many of the masked prediction subproblems MDMs train on are computationally intractable, while autoregressive models (ARMs) trained in a suitable order avoid these hard cases. They then show that simple adaptive inference strategies that choose the next tokens to unmask based on the model’s own logits can systematically sidestep hard subproblems at sampling time, yielding dramatic empirical gains on logic puzzles (e.g., Sudoku from <7% to ≈90% accuracy) and consistent improvements on text generation metrics.
strengths: Introduces a clear conceptual lens—“train for the worst, plan for the best”—that disentangles training-time task hardness from inference-time order flexibility in MDMs.

Provides average-case hardness constructions (latents-and-observations families) that go beyond cryptographic or worst-case examples and target typical masking regimes encountered during MDM reverse processes.

Reformulates the MDM loss as an average over all permutation-conditioned AR factorizations, connecting order-agnostic training to the hardness of π-learners and setting up the adaptive inference rationale.
Proposes a simple but effective Top-K probability margin oracle for adaptive ordering that is demonstrably better than Top-K certainty in settings with near-ties (e.g., Sudoku).

Thorough comparisons across domains: controlled L&O synthetic data, large-scale text (SlimPajama scaling studies, generative perplexity), and challenging reasoning tasks (Sudoku, Zebra puzzles), including a harder Sudoku out-of-distribution test.

Strong baselines, including ARMs with and without supervised ordering (teacher forcing), and attention to inductive biases (using learnable positional embeddings to avoid RoPE’s left-to-right bias in permutation experiments).
Clear evidence that adaptive inference improves both sample quality on text (generative perplexity) and task accuracy in logic puzzles, with analyses of entropy to check diversity.

Well-motivated decomposition of training versus inference issues, with intuitive figures illustrating how order-agnostic training faces a diversity of subproblems and how adaptive inference can route around the hard ones.
Precise formalization of forward/reverse MDM processes and a clean statement (Proposition 2.1) linking the objective to masked infilling across all masks/permutations.
Theoretical sections are scaffolded with definitions/propositions and pointers to appendices for full proofs.
Addresses a central open question for discrete diffusion: why MDMs underperform ARMs on text likelihood yet can excel in reasoning/planning tasks, and what to do about it.

Demonstrates large, training-free gains from adaptive inference that rival or surpass task-specific ARMs with explicit ordering supervision, highlighting practical value and potential for broader adoption.
weaknesses: The hardness results, while insightful, rely on assumptions (e.g., SLPN, 1RSB cavity predictions, stability barriers) and specific planted/structured distributions; the strength of implications for natural text remains indirect.

The claim that “MDMs train on computationally intractable subproblems” risks overgeneralization; results hold for specific masking rates and families and do not imply universal intractability across realistic corpora.

Generative perplexity is measured via an external evaluator (LLaMA-7B); while common, this metric has known caveats and does not directly assess true likelihood. More triangulation (e.g., human/LLM ratings or alternative evaluators) would strengthen the case.
Limited ablations on the adaptive oracle design: sensitivity to K, dynamic K schedules, entropy-based or calibration-aware scores, or mutual-information surrogates are not explored.

Compute and latency overheads of adaptive inference (e.g., extra sorting, margin computation, number of steps) are not systematically benchmarked against vanilla MDM and AR decoding, especially on long sequences.

For text scaling, π-learner results compellingly show performance degrades as permutations deviate from identity, but the link to computational hardness (vs. distributional mismatch to natural left-to-right text) is suggestive rather than definitive.

Some important training and evaluation details, particularly for logic puzzle models and the LLaDA 8B experiments, are deferred to appendices; summarizing key hyperparameters and decoding settings in the main text would aid reproducibility.
A few figures report large relative gains without error bars or confidence intervals; uncertainty reporting would help interpret robustness.

While adaptive inference is related to prior MDM samplers (e.g., Zheng et al., first-hitting; planned denoising; search-augmented inference), head-to-head quantitative comparisons are limited. More direct empirical contrasts, especially with recent neurosymbolic steering (e.g., SearchDiff) and hybrid-noise/self-correction methods, would position the contribution more precisely.

The growing literature on learned decoding orders (any-order AR, learned feature-wise schedules for MDMs) suggests complementary approaches; a discussion of synergies (e.g., learning state-dependent orders during training versus adaptive at inference) would be valuable.
reviews.questions:
1. How sensitive are the adaptive results to the choice of K and to dynamic K schedules across timesteps? Have you explored annealing K or budget-aware selection (e.g., commit fewer tokens when uncertainties are close)?
2. Did you evaluate alternative uncertainty measures beyond Top-K and margin (e.g., entropy, top-2 probability ratio, expected calibration error–adjusted scores, mutual-information surrogates)? How do they compare on Sudoku/text?
3. What is the runtime and memory overhead of adaptive inference relative to vanilla MDM and AR decoding on long sequences (e.g., 2k–4k tokens)? Please include per-sample latency and FLOPs.
4. For the π-learner experiments, could some degradation be attributed to model misspecification (e.g., positional encodings, inductive biases) rather than intrinsic computational hardness? Any ablations with different positional schemes or local-permutation curricula?
5. On Sudoku/Zebra, how fair is the comparison to ARMs with ordering teacher forcing in terms of training compute (epochs, tokens) and hyperparameter tuning? Can you provide training budget parity or sensitivity?
6. How robust is Top-K margin to miscalibration (overconfident logits)? Did you try temperature scaling or other calibration methods to improve order selection?
7. Could you report uncertainty (error bars) for key results (e.g., Sudoku/Zebra accuracy, generative perplexity) over multiple seeds, and include success@N if relevant?
reviews.assessment: This paper makes a timely and impactful contribution to our understanding of discrete diffusion models. The theoretical results, while assumption-dependent, give a cogent explanation for why order-agnostic MDM training can face hard subproblems that ARMs avoid under good orderings. The empirical section convincingly shows that inference-time adaptivity—implemented with a simple Top-K margin heuristic—can largely neutralize these hard cases in practice, leading to dramatic improvements on reasoning-heavy tasks and consistent gains on text generation quality as measured by external evaluators. The work is clearly written, well-motivated, and addresses an important question for the field. Some aspects could be strengthened—especially head-to-head comparisons with other adaptive/steering methods, more detailed runtime analyses, and richer ablations on the oracle design—but the core message and results are strong. I recommend acceptance.
